ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mock chicken mutter curry. FOODON_03315595 (curry food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM (vegetable curry) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for mock-meat pea curry.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for mock chicken mutter curry or a sufficiently close parent food concept.

main_ingredients: mock chicken (soy protein / textured vegetable protein)

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant portion of mock chicken mutter curry is approximately 300 g, typically served with rice or bread such as naan or roti.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry (composite estimate adapted for vegetarian mock-chicken variant with green peas); cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Chicken curry and Green peas entries; and USDA FoodData Central — Textured Vegetable Protein (TVP) cooked
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for mock chicken mutter curry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for Indian chicken curry (used as structural template for the curry base: spices, oil, tomato, onion) with USDA FoodData Central data for cooked textured vegetable protein (TVP) substituted for chicken, and ICMR-NIN data for cooked green peas. Proportions assumed: ~35% mock chicken TVP, ~20% green peas, ~45% curry sauce base. Energy and macros are weighted composites. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of legume-based and soy-protein curry dishes; analogous estimate based on green pea curry and soy protein entries in the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mock chicken mutter curry. GI estimated at 38 based on: (1) green peas GI ~48 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr); (2) soy protein / TVP GI ~25–30 (University of Sydney GI Database); (3) tomato-onion curry base with oil contributing negligible glycemic load. Weighted composite GI estimated at ~38 (low category). GL per 300 g serving = (38 × 8.9 × 3) / 100 = 10.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full restaurant portion of mock chicken mutter curry may contribute approximately 960 mg sodium (~42% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~38) driven by soy protein and green peas; moderate dietary fibre (3.1 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mock chicken mutter curry has a low GI (~38) and moderate fibre content, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with a low-GI staple such as basmati rice or whole-wheat roti, and portion size is kept to one standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, low saturated fat (1.4 g per 100 g), high plant protein from soy TVP and green peas
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mock chicken mutter curry is cholesterol-free and low in saturated fat, with plant-based protein from soy and peas that may support LDL reduction; however, sodium content should be monitored in individuals with cardiovascular disease and concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mock Chicken Mutter is a fully vegetarian Indian curry dish in which textured vegetable protein (TVP) or soy-based mock chicken pieces replace conventional chicken, simmered together with green peas (mutter) in a spiced tomato-onion gravy. The dish is a staple offering at Indian vegetarian restaurants, including establishments such as Jewel of India in Singapore's Boat Quay district. The curry base is built from a sofrito of onion, garlic, and ginger cooked in oil, enriched with ground cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, and chilli, then finished with tomato and optionally a small amount of cream or yogurt. The mock chicken pieces absorb the spiced gravy and develop a tender, slightly chewy texture that closely mimics the mouthfeel of braised chicken. The green peas add sweetness, colour, and dietary fibre. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of plant protein (~8.2 g per 100 g), dietary fibre (~3.1 g per 100 g), and iron (~2.1 mg per 100 g), while being cholesterol-free and low in saturated fat. Its low glycemic index (~38) makes it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations (~320 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal-certified in most Indian vegetarian restaurant contexts and is suitable for lacto-vegetarians. It is typically served with basmati rice, naan, or roti as part of a main meal.
common_user_queries: Is mock chicken mutter curry vegetarian?
What is mock chicken made of in Indian curry?

Can I eat mock chicken mutter if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for mock chicken mutter curry exists in ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central, or HPB Singapore Foodo. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing ICMR-NIN chicken curry base data with USDA TVP cooked data and ICMR-NIN green peas data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for soy protein and green peas. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation methodology.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Values should be treated as indicative and updated if a direct measurement becomes available. 2. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA TVP source entries used for this composite estimate. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish; soy TVP and green peas contain no vitamin B12 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked TVP and ICMR-NIN for green peas. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as plant-based curry dishes without fortified ingredients contain no vitamin D — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked TVP. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in this dish — confirmed zero by ingredient composition. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish — naturally zero; no animal-derived ingredients contribute cholesterol. 7. GI estimated at 38 (low) based on analogous University of Sydney GI Database entries for soy protein and green peas; GL per 300 g serving = 10.1. 8. The food name in the input JSON contains a typographical error ('Chiken' instead of 'Chicken'); the canonical English name has been corrected to 'Mock Chicken Mutter'. The misspelling is preserved as an alias. 9. This dish is served at Jewel of India, an Indian vegetarian restaurant at Boat Quay, Singapore, at SGD 16.90 per portion.
